figures.caption: Figure 2 | Pump network synthesis problem (Section 4). (a) An 𝐿-level pump configuration where each level consists of pumps arranged in series-parallel, with level-specific parameters for speed, flow, power, and pressure rise. (b) Normalized objective function contours and feasible region for a single-level optimization instance of this problem.
Table 3 | Training configurations by problem category. 𝐺: rollouts per prompt.

Figure 1 | AutoOR Overview. (a) We instantiate ground-truth solver code with new parameters, producing synthetic problems that are correct by construction. These are then backtranslated into natural language descriptions. (b) This data enables RL training for the reverse direction: given a description, the policy generates solver code and receives execution feedback as the reward signal.

Figure 3 | Curriculum RL strategy for non-linear optimization. Phase 1 provides Gekko solver syntax and all constraints as privileged information and trains on easy non-linear problems. Phase 2 removes this guidance and continues training on the same distribution. Phase 3 trains on hard problems from the full non-linear distribution. Each phase produces a policy with sufficient solvability on the next distribution for GRPO to generate learning signal.

Table 1 | Main results (pass@1) across established and new benchmarks. Accuracy (%) reported. AutoOR 8B is trained with GRPO on synthetic data from our pipeline. For non-linear problems, all baseline models score near zero. † = introduced in this work. ⋄= sc@3 metric.
Table 2 | Comparison with learning-based methods on established benchmarks. Accuracy (%) from published results (- indicates unavailable results).1
Table 4 | Reward components by problem category (additive).
